quick_facts.label: What it is
quick_facts.value: A Master in Management (MiM) is a postgraduate business/management degree. Overseas programmes vary substantially by school: for example, INSEAD describes a 14-month full-time MiM, while HEC Paris describes its MiM as a two-year programme.

quick_facts.label: Typical candidate profile
quick_facts.value: It is commonly designed for applicants early in their careers. HEC Paris states that it welcomes applicants with little or no professional experience; London Business School targets recent graduates with no more than two years of postgraduate work experience.

quick_facts.label: GMAT connection
quick_facts.value: GMAT is accepted or required by many MiM programmes, but policies differ. INSEAD requires a GMAT or GRE score; HEC Paris requires a GMAT, GRE or TageMage score report but states that no minimum score is required to apply; London Business School accepts GMAT or GRE.

quick_facts.label: Academic entry baseline
quick_facts.value: A completed bachelor’s degree or equivalent is a common baseline. INSEAD requires an undergraduate degree or equivalent, while HEC Paris specifies a bachelor’s degree of at least three years/180 ECTS for its international admissions route.

quick_facts.label: Illustrative tuition context
quick_facts.value: Published tuition is school- and intake-specific and excludes or varies in treatment of living and travel costs. For the 2026 intake, London Business School lists £52,950 tuition; INSEAD lists €57,870 for its MiM Class of 2028; HEC Paris lists indicative two-year fees of €57,700 plus stated additional charges.

sections.heading: Who a Master in Management abroad is for
paragraphs: A MiM is generally an early-career postgraduate management route rather than a replacement for an experienced-professional MBA. At INSEAD, full-time professional experience is not required, although business exposure is valued. HEC Paris accepts applicants of all nationalities with little or no professional experience, and its international route requires a bachelor’s degree of at least three years (180 ECTS) in any discipline. London Business School similarly positions its MiM for recent graduates, permitting a maximum of two years of postgraduate work experience.

Applicants should treat eligibility as programme-specific. Academic strength, an accepted admissions test, English-language evidence where applicable, and the school’s definition of allowable work experience can all affect eligibility. For example, London Business School requires a bachelor’s degree and says applicants must take either the GMAT or GRE before applying; HEC Paris requests degree records, transcripts, a CV, an accepted test score and, where required, English-language test evidence.
bullets: A bachelor’s degree is the usual entry qualification; confirm equivalence and minimum academic standard with each university.
GMAT can be a central application component, but it is not a universal MiM requirement and some schools also accept GRE or other tests.
Recent graduates and applicants with limited experience are the core audience at many programmes; count internships and post-graduation employment according to each school’s rule.

For Indian applicants, also check document translation requirements, English-test exemptions, visa rules, proof-of-funds requirements and recognition requirements directly with the destination-country authorities and university.

sections.heading: Duration and study format
paragraphs: There is no single standard duration for an overseas MiM. INSEAD’s MiM is advertised as a full-time 14-month programme, followed by a six-month internship period. HEC Paris states that its MiM fee schedule applies to a two-year programme. These examples show why applicants should compare the academic calendar, internship or gap-year structure, campus locations and any optional extensions—not only the degree title.

A longer programme can provide more time for electives, internships and local recruiting, while a shorter programme can reduce time out of work. Neither structure is inherently better: the practical fit depends on the target country, financing plan, language preparation and intended career path.
bullets: Check whether the published length includes an internship, exchange, optional gap year or only taught terms.
Confirm the programme’s intake month and whether attendance is full-time and campus-based.
Use the university’s current academic calendar rather than relying on historic course brochures.

sections.heading: What students study
paragraphs: MiM curricula normally build broad business foundations before allowing specialisation. INSEAD describes a five-period curriculum using case studies, simulations, company visits and group projects, with a professional exposure period of four to six months. At HEC Paris, the general-management phase combines core management courses and electives; its published examples include corporate finance, financial accounting, data science, marketing management, operations and supply chain, organisational behavior, strategy, company law and financial markets.

This means the degree can suit graduates from engineering, science, humanities, social sciences and commerce backgrounds who want structured preparation for business roles. However, the precise balance of finance, analytics, sustainability, languages, consulting projects and electives differs by university and may change by intake year.
bullets: Typical foundations: accounting, finance, economics, marketing, strategy, operations, organisational behaviour and data/analytics.
Applied components may include cases, simulations, company projects, visits and internships.
Compare mandatory courses, elective availability, language teaching, exchange options and assessment style before applying.

sections.heading: How to approach admission
paragraphs: A competitive MiM application is usually assessed holistically rather than through GMAT alone. INSEAD lists academic performance, a GMAT/GRE score and an undergraduate degree among its criteria, alongside the ability to contribute and international motivation. Its application process includes an online application, written and video questions, pre-selection and an alumnus interview for pre-selected candidates. HEC Paris lists official transcripts, degree or enrolment evidence, CV, GMAT/GRE/TageMage score report and English-language evidence where applicable among supporting documents.

Build the application around a coherent early-career objective: explain why management education is needed now, show evidence of academic readiness and teamwork/leadership potential, and connect the programme’s curriculum and recruiting geography to the intended path. Verify the exact test format, score-report delivery method, recommendation requirements, rounds and deadlines on the school’s official admissions page for the intended intake; these details change regularly.
bullets: Prepare academic transcripts, degree/current-enrolment documents, CV and identity documents early.
Book GMAT/GRE early enough to allow a retake if needed and to send the official score report by the school’s deadline.
Plan for essays, recommendations, English-test evidence where required and an interview if shortlisted.
Apply in a round that leaves enough time for admission decisions, financing, visa processing and accommodation.

sections.heading: Costs, funding and budgeting
paragraphs: Costs must be evaluated as a full cost of attendance, not tuition alone. As published for the indicated intakes, INSEAD lists €57,870 tuition for the MiM Class of 2028, London Business School lists £52,950 tuition for the 2026 intake, and HEC Paris lists indicative two-year fees of €57,700, with additional student-service and administrative fees in its schedule and an extra tuition charge for international students. These amounts are dated examples, may change, and are not directly comparable because programme duration and included services differ.

Budget separately for accommodation, food, local transport, health insurance, visa/immigration charges, flights, deposits, books or equipment not included, test fees and programme travel. HEC Paris, for example, gives an approximate living-cost figure of €1,500 per month in 2025; it is an institutional estimate rather than a universal student budget. Explore institution-specific merit and need-based scholarships, external scholarships and education-loan terms before accepting an offer.
bullets: Record the tuition year, currency, programme length and every mandatory fee when comparing offers.
Check instalment dates and non-refundable deposits before committing; cash-flow timing can matter as much as total tuition.
Do not convert historical fee figures into INR and treat them as a final budget: use current exchange rates and add a contingency.
Read scholarship conditions carefully, including whether awards are automatic, need-based, merit-based, renewable or applied against tuition only.

sections.heading: Career and further-study options
paragraphs: A MiM can lead to entry-level and early-career roles across consulting, financial services, technology and diversified industries, as well as functions such as project management, finance, sales and marketing, supply chain and operations. INSEAD presents these as possible career directions rather than guaranteed outcomes. Its published 2024 employment data reports that 95% of the MiM’24 class received an offer within three months of graduation; this is a historical school-reported outcome, not a prediction for a future applicant or a guarantee of employment.

A MiM can also be followed later by an MBA or another postgraduate qualification once professional experience has been gained. London Business School explicitly describes its MiM as a possible stepping stone to an MBA, while noting that candidates must still meet the later programme’s entry requirements. Career outcomes depend on the student’s prior experience, local work authorisation, language ability, hiring market, interview performance and the recruiting practices of the target country.
bullets: Target roles through internships, employer presentations, alumni networking, career coaching and campus recruiting where available.
For overseas jobs, assess work-authorisation routes and local-language expectations before choosing a country or programme.
Consider a later MBA after building meaningful work experience if the goal is a mid-career career switch or leadership progression.
